title: Chapter 12: The Distraction
content: The plan was simple. Simple plans were the only kind I could execute.

Step one: Create a distraction powerful enough to draw Derek and his two recruits away from Floor 8. Step two: Get inside Apartment 8A. Step three: Find Jessica Park and get her out. Step four: Don't die.
Steps one through four had varying degrees of feasibility, with step four being the most uncertain.
"Sound device," Luna said, pointing at my wrist. "System gave before."

She was right—back when we'd first scouted Derek, the system had awarded me a "Distraction Module" as a daily bonus: a palm-sized electronic device that emitted a repeating audio pattern designed to attract zombies. I'd never used it. The idea of *attracting* zombies had seemed suicidal when I was a lone gamer with a can of tuna. But now I had Luna. And Luna wasn't afraid of her own kind.

"We set it off on Floor 7," I said, sketching the plan on my notebook. "The sound draws every zombie on the lower floors toward the source. Derek hears the commotion, thinks it's a threat or an opportunity—he takes his people to investigate. While he's gone, we slip into 8A and grab Jessica."

"What if he doesn't go?" Sarah asked. She'd insisted on being part of the planning session, sitting at the kitchen table with her arms crossed, contributing tactical observations that were sharper than anything I could have produced. A week of surviving Derek had given her a Ph.D. in reading dangerous men.
"He'll go," I said. "Derek's paranoid. Any unusual sound, he has to check it. It's a control thing—he can't stand not knowing what's happening in his building."
"And if he takes Jessica with him as leverage?"
I hadn't considered that. Sarah saw it in my face.

"He won't," she said, answering her own question. "She's not valuable to him as leverage. She's just... labor. Someone to clean and cook. He'd leave her behind because he doesn't think she'd ever have the courage to run."
There was something in her voice—a bitter recognition. She'd been in Jessica's position. She knew exactly how Derek thought, because she'd spent a week learning every terrifying corner of his mind.
"Okay," I said. "Then we work with that. He leaves, she stays, we get her out."
Luna held up one finger. "One problem. Three floors of dead things between us and Floor 8. Distraction pulls them to Floor 7, but not all. Some will stay."
"I'll handle the stragglers," Luna said.
"You'll handle—how?"

She stood up from her chair and walked to the center of the room. Then, with no warning, she moved—not walked, *moved*—a blur of motion that crossed the apartment in under a second, her hand stopping an inch from my face, fingers splayed, the concrete wall behind me cracked from the air pressure of her passage.
"I handle," she said.

Sarah's jaw had dropped. I'd seen Luna's speed before, but never demonstrated so casually, so *precisely*. She could have killed me before I blinked, and instead she'd stopped with the delicacy of a surgeon.
"That's... unsettling," Sarah managed.
"That's our edge," I said. "Luna's faster and stronger than anything else in this building. Including Derek's gun."
"Guns are still guns," Sarah said, recovering. "Speed doesn't stop bullets."

"No. But distraction does." I pulled the device from my backpack—a matte black disc about the size of a hockey puck, with a single button and a timer display. "We lure them in, split them up, and pick them off. Classic aggro management."
"Aggro management," Sarah repeated. "You're treating this like a video game."

"I'm treating this like a video game because video games taught me how to handle encounters with limited resources, split enemy groups, and control engagement distance. Call it what you want—it works."
She looked like she wanted to argue but couldn't find the flaw. Pre-med precision meeting gamer logic—it was an unlikely combination, but it held.
"One more thing," I said. "Who stays here to watch our supplies and—frankly—make sure no one sneaks in while we're gone?"
"I'll stay," Sarah said immediately. "I'm not combat-ready, and I'll slow you down. But I can hold a door and I can scream if something comes through it."
It was practical. It was smart. And it was the first time she'd volunteered for anything since arriving.
"Okay. You're on door duty. Luna and I go in, get Jessica, get out. No heroics, no extended engagements."
"Right," Sarah said, with a look that suggested she'd heard that particular lie before—from herself, probably, on the night she'd decided to stay with Derek instead of running.
I chose not to examine that parallel too closely.
At 3:00 PM, Derek's nap time, I placed the distraction device on the Floor 7 landing, set the timer for three minutes, and Luna and I retreated to the Floor 9 stairwell door.

The wait was the worst part. Three minutes of silence, broken only by my heartbeat and Luna's slow, deliberate breathing. She was calm—not the calm of resignation, but the calm of a predator in her element. This was her world now: dark hallways, distant moaning, the taste of violence in the air.
"How do you feel?" I whispered, more to fill the silence than out of genuine curiosity.
"Alive," she said. Which was either the most honest or the most heartbreaking answer she could have given.
The device activated.

The sound it made was... wrong. Not loud—at least, not in the way I'd expected. It was a *pattern*, a rhythmic pulsing that seemed to bypass the ears and go straight to something deeper, something primitive. I felt it in my teeth, in my spine, in the base of my skull where the lizard brain lived.
And from every floor below us, the zombies responded.

The moaning intensified—not aimless, but *directed*. Footsteps multiplied. The stairwell became a highway of dead feet shuffling downward, drawn by the siren song of the device like moths to a flame that burned in frequencies I couldn't perceive.
"On our floor?" I asked Luna.
She listened. "Clear. Most went down. Some stayed on 8... in apartments. Not the hallway."
Derek's voice, distant and furious: "What the hell is that? Rick, check it out. Maria, stay with the girl."
Three people. Two investigating. One guarding Jessica.
Better than three. But not good.
"Go," I said.

Luna moved. I followed as fast as I could, which was roughly a third of her speed, but the hallway was clear—the distraction had done its job, pulling the shamblers to Floor 7 like iron filings to a magnet. We reached 8A in under a minute.
The door was closed. Not locked—Derek had gotten lazy about locks now that he had followers, relying on numbers instead of security.
I opened it.

The woman inside—Jessica—was sitting on the kitchen floor, back against the cabinets, wrapped in a blanket that was more holes than fabric. She was young, maybe nineteen, with bleached-blonde hair and eyes that had seen too much. When the door opened, she screamed.
Luna was beside her in an instant—not threatening, not grabbing, just *present*, crouching at her level, those dark brown eyes offering something I couldn't name.
"Safe," Luna said. "We are safe. Coming with us."
Jessica stared at her—at the pale skin, the too-still posture, the predator's grace contained in a young woman's body.
"You're one of them," Jessica whispered.
"I was. Now I'm not. Come."
And like Sarah before her, Jessica looked at this impossible stranger offering an impossible promise, and she decided to believe.
She took Luna's hand.
From somewhere in the building, the distraction device clicked off. The siren stopped. And from Floor 7, the sound of Derek's voice, agitated and returning:
"Find whoever did that. FIND THEM."
Time was up.
